The box arrives on a rainy Tuesday morning. Leon is not at home, and by the time he gets back, the box is already waiting for him with a cheery note attached.

WHILE YOU WERE GONE, ROBO DELIVERS!

There was a pathetic attempt to hide it behind a houseplant, but most of it is in sight. Go figure, a box large enough to fit a human being is too big to hide behind a dying fern.

(See the end of the work for notes.)

The box arrives on a rainy Tuesday morning.

Leon is not at home. He has practice at ten, followed by a gym session and a photoshoot for a team sponsor. By the time he gets back, the box has already been left on his doorstep with a cheery note attached.

There was a pathetic attempt by the courier to hide the package behind a houseplant, but most of it is in sight. Go figure, a box large enough to fit a human being is too big to hide behind a dying fern.

Leon struggles to drag it inside. There are handles at the top, but the whole thing is pretty heavy, and the shape is cumbersome, wide and flat. He’s sweating by the time he gets it into his living room and starts to rip the tape.

Inside the unmarked cardboard box, he finds an instruction manual that he promptly throws away, alongside another container. This one is much sleeker, exactly what one would expect for how much money Leon spent. The surface material is smooth and reflective, with rounded corners and a compact feel. There is a logo at the top of the box.

NHR: Neo Humanoid Robotics

Leon skims the box with his fingers, trying to find how it opens. He glances at the manual on the floor, but he's too lazy to pick it up. It shouldn't be too hard anyway. That's the whole point of these fancy new robots. They're supposed to make your life easier.

In the end, he discovers a button inside one of the O's in the logo and presses it without thinking about what's next. The box releases a smooth gust of air as the top lid swings back and the side walls fall to the floor. Leon rushes to his coffee table and shoves it out of the way just before it gets hit.

Inside the container is a human being wearing a grey t-shirt and loose jeans. His hair is mid-length, a mixture of blonde and ginger. Leon can tell it's soft just from looking at it. He has a close-cut beard that's a little patchy, as if he can't grow it out properly but wants to give it a go anyway. Although he's in a hunched position, Leon can still tell he's big, with long legs and wide shoulders.

The human inside the box is not a human at all, of course, but the result of a thoughtless purchase.

Leon got it on a whim months ago, a few beers deep and no one around to call him out on his dumb idea. It was supposed to be a gift for himself, a band-aid on the wound of yet another disappointing playoff run. Tired and lonely, he had been the prime target for Instagram ads.

Leon didn't know much about human robots when he got it. Still doesn't, truth be told.

He knows they exist, of course, and that they're becoming increasingly popular as the technology develops, but he had never considered getting one for himself before. The whole idea kinda freaked him out. Something that looks human, acts human, can have human-like thoughts, yet is not human? Living in his house? Weird.

And yet here is one, bought by him. Not just that, but made for him.

He knows that making such a large purchase with so little thought was, objectively, dumb. That he's the equivalent of a Facebook mom who gets scammed every month by unbelievable product claims.

BUY THIS DEVICE AND YOU'LL LOSE 50 POUNDS, FIND THE LOVE OF YOUR LIFE AND ACHIEVE NIRVANA!

The ad will say in a friendly, bubble font, and he's getting his credit card out before he's even finished reading, desperate to have all his problems solved by capitalism.

So yes, he's aware he's an idiot. That he probably should have done at least one Google search before spending — he doesn't even remember how much, but enough that he had to call his bank to unlock his account. Alas, the past is the past.

To Leon's credit, the company had looked legit. Decent website, good product photos, and the type of messaging that hit him right where it hurt.

Your Companion Machine Droid is not just another robot in your home. It is a true partner who understands your every need, want and desire. Made from a detailed profile quiz, the CmD personality is designed for you and you only, including moments of pleasure and delight that will surprise you at every turn.

At Neo Humanoid Robotics, we strive to create droids that feel more human than real humans. The CmD is made to give you not just what you want, but what you need. Programmed to know a variety of skills, it will bring a warmth to every home, with home-cooked meals, skillfull massages and ready feedback on the topics you're interested in.

Fill out your personality quiz now and get ready for the next chapter of your life.

So Leon did what the well-designed website page asked. He filled out the quiz. Page after page of increasingly more personal and inane questions, designed to unpick his personality into character traits scientists could reverse engineer, all in the hope of finding something good at the end.

Some questions made sense to him. Savoury or sweet? Are you a morning dove or a night owl? Do you prefer bottoming, topping or switching?

He blushed his way through some of the more raunchy ones, but answered them to the best of his abilities. The initial advertising hadn't said much about sex, but it seemed they were keen to deliver on the understanding your every need promise.

Other questions left him scratching his head. What do you think about when you're not thinking about anything? If you were a fish, what type of fish would you be? Rate these cheeses on a scale from 1 to 5, with 1 being 'disgusting' and 5 being 'something you'd be willing to eat exclusively for the rest of your life'.

At one point, Leon seriously considered closing the page and giving up on the whole thing. The more questions he answered, the more sober he got, the more he realised this whole thing was a dumb idea. He was barely home with his current schedule. What was he even supposed to do with a custom-made bot that was always at home, ready to answer his every beck and call?

Well, besides the obvious, anyway.

The whole thing was embarrassing. Profoundly, soul-crushingly embarrassing. Was he that much of a lonely bastard that he needed to buy companionship? It wasn't even about sex, which somehow only made it worse.

Leon was a famous hockey player in Canada. He could get laid any time he wanted, if that was what he wanted, but that was the issue, wasn't it? Leon didn't want to get laid, or at least he didn't want just sex. He was lonely, and he was being promised love on a platter.

It was a good Instagram ad, he would give them that.

He entered his credit card info without checking the shipping date, didn't see it until there was an email in his inbox promising delivery in three to six months.

"Fuck," he said out loud to absolutely no one. Three to six months? Did he just get scammed? God, he was never going to hear the end of this from his accountant.

A quick—certainly untimely—web search revealed that Neo Human0id Robotics was not a scam company, though its full legitimacy was up for debate.

Custom-made bots take time to make, but this one is certainly worth it! Read a cheery review by a user named Big Tits Susan.

Another user review by someone called Steve-O was more detailed. I've never owned a bot with so much personality. He seems to know a lot more than I expected. My friends say I got a good deal here, and I do enjoy having him around, but I worry he'll find me lacking one day.

A final review read Best fuck of my life!! Followed by seventeen different emojis and three very explicit photos that he quickly scrolled away from.

Alright, Leon thought, and decided he'd had enough internet for a day.

The one good thing about the long delivery time was that he didn't have to worry about the bot arriving while he was in Germany for the summer.

He pictured it for a moment. The package waiting on his doorstep for long enough that one of his neighbours noticed and called him. "Hey man, the sex robot you ordered is getting mouldy outside. You want me to bring it in?"

Leon fumbling his way through the conversation.

"It's not like that," he would say. His neighbour wouldn't believe him, which was only reasonable. He wouldn't believe himself either.

Three to six months was fine, though. Leon would be back by then. He could even set up an alarm to ensure he would be home when the bot arrived.

Obviously, he never did.

What actually happened was that he forgot about the whole fucking thing.

He went to Germany, saw his family, ate a lot of pasta, and fucked a couple of women and a couple of men, sometimes at the same time. He spent a lot of time in the sun and only remembered to wear sunscreen sometimes. He was never alone, a rotating cast of friends and acquaintances crashing at his place, eating his food and following him from place to place, bills all taken care of by Leon and his fuck you NHL money.

It wasn't a bad summer. A bit repetitive, maybe, but it got his mind off hockey, which was what he needed. It made him forget the slog that is playing day in, day out. Getting his body smashed against the boards by guys who couldn't find a puck inside their own ass. Scoring three goals just to have his defence let in four. A miserable end to another miserable season.

But summer was nice. Warm and lazy. Filled with people who mostly didn't know or didn't care about hockey.

Leon enjoyed his time away. He forgot why he was depressed in the first place and came back to Edmonton full of energy, or however much energy he could muster, which on him always looked like a tank half full of gas, half full of rage. Whatever.

He forgot all about his expensive purchase until it was in his living room, lying on his floor.

And now what?

Leon stares at it for what feels like an eternity before he finally plucks up the courage to find the instruction manual. He has no clue how to turn this thing on, and he's not about to risk finding out on his own. He's already made enough poor decisions to last him a lifetime.

C0NN0R BOT H97: YOUR GUIDE TO A HAPPY LIFE WITH YOUR COMPANION ;)

A little bit on the nose, but okay. Leon flips through the pages, ignoring most of the safety warnings and skipping to the GETTING STARTED section. The manual is quite chunky, with most of it devoted to advice on how to make the most of his very expensive toy. He figures he can get to that later or maybe never.

It's a companion robot. How hard can it be to figure out?

The setup experience is about as boring and time-consuming as setting up a new washing machine. There's a charging cable between the robot's legs that Leon feels awkward about picking up.

He finds his robot to be as heavy and malleable as a real human being. His legs spread with ease, no resistance to them. Leon feels like a right creep. He tries to step away as soon as he's got the cable, only to remember he needs to find the charging hole.

There's an obvious joke to be made there that Leon is ignoring, even in the sanctity of his own mind. This whole experience is already damaging enough to his psyche as it is.

The robot's charging hole is on the back of its neck, right below its identifying barcode. Leon gently pushes the hair there away, finding it as soft as he imagined. The barcode is about six centimetres long and four in height. Leon knows all human-looking robots must have one, with heavy penalties for anyone found tampering with them.

Leon plugs in the cable, then lets the robot drop to the floor. He briefly considers moving it to the couch before discarding the idea. It's fine. According to the instructions, the first charging cycle should take all night. He can leave it there and unplug it in the morning.

With that decision made, He tries to go about his night routine as he normally would. He heats the container labelled DINNER #2, a chicken ravioli creation that he finds rather dry, and adds a healthy dose of mustard to make it more palatable. He grabs a Diet Coke with lots of ice and brings his food to the living room to watch whatever true crime documentary Netflix has on offer that week.

He doesn't consider how awkward it is to watch television with a humanoid robot lying on the floor until he actually does it.

It just looks so… real. Leon knows robotics has improved leaps and bounds since Amazon released their first bots a couple of years ago, but it's still astonishing to see it up close. Expensive robots like the one Leon got, in the mid-five figures, are rare to come across in the wild. The ones you see behind counters or doing small tasks are often lumbering, stilted things. Some don't even have skin, making no pretence of looking human.

This one is about as real as they come. It even has wrinkles and acne scars. Its cheekbones are a little gaunt, as if it struggles to keep itself fed properly, which is a crazy thought to have about a robot that has no need for sustenance except a charge cycle every day.

The robot's actual body is almost underwhelming in its normalcy. It's definitely not what Leon expected when he clicked purchase on what most would call a sex bot, but which he is insisting on calling a companionship bot.

It looks like one of the countless guys Leon meets every time he gets on ice. Tall, sturdy, with thick thighs and a nice ass.

"Huh," Leon says as the realisation hits him.

It's a hockey body. He got a robot with a hockey body.

It's the type of body Leon refuses to look at when he's in the locker room because he doesn't want to make it weird for the other guys. The type he finds himself drawn to when he's drunk and anonymous at a club, who he's jerked off to in the privacy of his bedroom more than once. The type he usually tries his damn hardest to ignore, now lying there on the floor of his living room, designed precisely for him.

With that thought, Leon picks up his dinner and goes to eat in his dining room. He really doesn't use that space enough.

He eats while staring at his phone, then decides an early night is just what he needs. He's walking past his living room, pointedly not looking, when he finds himself looking anyway.

It's a bit of a sad sight.

His robot, lying on the floor and hooked onto the wall. Its head is in an awkward position. If it were a real person, it would definitely wake up with a crick in its neck from how it slept. Leon feels bad for leaving it there, so with a sigh, he marches up and lifts it, dragging it onto the couch. He leaves the head on one of the cushions, turned slightly to the side so the cable at the back doesn't get scrunched up.

One of his hands strays from the robot's neck to its shirt. The material is as soft as its hair.

Leon will have to get him a wardrobe. Will it want to wear different clothes? The thought of not changing outfits every day seems pretty foul to him. Will it have a preference as to what it wears? Eyeing it like this, Leon's pretty sure most of his own clothes will fit it, but maybe it won't enjoy wearing Leon's leftovers. Maybe it wants all new clothes.

Then again, Leon's got a lot of money and not a whole lot he spends it on. He can afford to buy a few new shirts and jeans. Maybe even some jackets, make sure it doesn't get cold. Edmonton winters are no joke. Even if it can't feel it, he wouldn't want his robot to be cold.

Although he's still quite sure he made the wrong decision buying the robot, as he stares at it, he can't help but think that maybe it won't be so bad after all. Maybe this won't be any weirder than buying a fancy gadget. It's just something to have at home and talk to, like the voice assistant on his phone.

It will be fine, really.

Despite being a morning person, Leon has always been a slow riser.

His mind needs a couple of minutes to catch up with his body, which tends to move on autopilot. Get up, stretch his back, take a piss, and head downstairs for breakfast. All in the dark because he finds artificial light offensive in the first hour of wakefulness.

He's still rubbing sleep from his eyes when he walks into the living room and finds someone sitting on the couch.

"Holy shit!" Leon shouts. He takes a step back on reflex, but instead of finding solid ground, his foot misses the stairs, and he falls on his ass like a cartoon character, his arms swinging in uselessly in the air.

Jesus fucking christ, that hurt a lot more than it does on ice.

"Oh, hello! I'm sorry, I didn't mean to startle you," says the man in Leon's living room. He rushes to Leon's side and pulls him up with a strong grip. Leon is too befuddled to stop him. "I'm Connor."

He's probably not a burglar, Leon thinks. He's pretty sure burglars don't usually introduce themselves.

Then again, it could be a very bizarre kidnapping attempt.

"Connor?" he asks, staring at the guy's face. It takes a couple of seconds, but realisation hits him with all the subtlety of being decked on ice. Conn0r.

"Yes, your NHR companion? You are Leon, correct?" the robot — his robot! — asks.

Leon squints at him and takes a step back, putting some distance between them now that he's partially awake and no longer lying on the stairs. "I am. How do you know my name?"

"All the information you provided in your personality quiz has been uploaded to my hard drive. I know your name, your favourite foods, how you take your coffee, your football team, your sexual preferences—"

"Alright, alright. You know everything about me."

Connor blinks at him. "No, not everything. I only know what was in your profile."

"Got it," Leon says. "So, I guess, it's nice to meet you… Connor?"

"Well, it's C0NN0R H97 actually, but you can call me Connor. That seems easier."

Leon nods. Connor it is.

They stare at each other for a moment, the silence growing more awkward by the second. This is Leon's robot. He should know what to say to him, but his lack of research on bots is glaring. Is he supposed to make small talk? Give Connor instructions? He looks around for inspiration.

"You want a house tour?" is what he ends up asking. It feels like the right thing to offer. If Connor is going to be living here with him, he should get to know the space.

"That would be nice," Connor replies, smiling at him.

Leon's home is bigger than average but modest by NHL standards. Since he lives on his own, he went for a detached house in the suburbs. A small garden for summer BBQ, a basement large enough to fit some gym equipment, and a couple of extra bedrooms for when his family are in town.

Connor seems to take it all in with the enthusiasm of someone who doesn't care in the slightest about interior design, but knows when to ooh and aah appropriately. "This is nice," he says about a couple of items Leon hired someone else to buy. Leon grunts in reply and quickly moves on.

The only room with any personality is the master bedroom. Leon has a couple of jerseys on the walls, as well as a few trophies. It feels arrogant to have them up on display, but he's not got enough to his name to warrant a dedicated trophy room, and he's not putting that stuff in the living room for everybody to see either.

Connor seems to find these more interesting than the vase he admired earlier. He stares at the jerseys in silence, as if cataloguing every minor detail about them.

"There were a couple of mentions of hockey on your profile, but it didn't say you played," Connor says, pointing to one of the framed jerseys.

Leon nods. That makes sense. He'd been deliberately vague when answering questions about his job, struck by a momentary panic that someone at NHR would be able to identify him and sell his story for a quick paycheck.

LEON DRAISAITL, STAR OF THE EDMONTON OILERS, BUYS SEX BOT ONLINE: LIVING A SAD, MISERABLE LIFE ON HIS OWN

So Leon listed hockey as one of his interests, which he assumed would be the case for anyone making a profile in Canada, but didn't mention that he played.

"For the Edmonton Oilers. That's where you are, right now, by the way. Edmonton."

"I know, I have GPS," Connor replies, turning to smile at him.

Right. He's a robot. Of course, he has GPS.

When Connor is done staring at the jerseys, he follows Leon out of the room. They're almost done with the tour now. The only rooms left are the guest rooms towards the back of the house.

Leon passes one of them in silence, then steps back and goes in.

"This could be your room," he says. It's empty and devoid of any personal touches. He's pretty sure his sister is the only person who's ever stayed here, his housekeeper promptly removing any signs of life as soon as she leaves.

"You want me to stay here?" Connor asks.

"Yeah, well. I know you don't need to sleep like a human, but it might still be nice to have your own space. You can use it when you need to charge, right?"

"Right," Connor agrees, looking dubious. "My own space."

"Yeah, I mean. I know it looks a bit generic right now." Understatement of the century. Leon has made a minimum effort to decorate the spaces he inhabits. The guest bedrooms, he hasn't even touched. "But we can get you some stuff if you'd like."

"Thank you," Connor says, looking at Leon with his big green eyes, as if no one has ever offered him such kindness.

It takes Leon a moment to realise that nobody would have. Connor is brand new to the world and people, though he comes programmed with all kinds of knowledge and expectations. Leon wonders if he's meeting them, then decides to push the thought away.

"So, what's next?" he asks once they're back in the living room.

"You're the one who got me. We can do whatever you like." At Leon's obvious blank look, Connor adds. "What do you usually do?"

That one is simple. It's game day, with Seattle flying in for a game at Rogers. That means practice at the training rink, media duties, followed by a quick nap back home before heading to the stadium. Leon is not quite sure how he feels about leaving his sentient robot at home by himself, though, but he can't figure out how to word his worry either.

"I would normally go to the rink," he says.

"Ah," Connor says. He looks like he was waiting for a different answer, but quickly recovers enough to add. "Don't let me stop you from doing what you normally do. I'll be here when you get back."

"Cool, cool. Well then, I'll just—" Leon points to the stairs. "Get dressed and head out," he says and then promptly bolts out of the room.

Practice is only at 11.30, but Leon is at the rink before 10. He's usually a punctual guy, but he hasn't come in early in years, ever since he built his own gym at home. His early appearance raises a few eyebrows, but no questions, which he's quietly grateful for.

Leon's got a good poker face when he lies, but he would rather avoid any conversations about his day altogether. He busies himself at the gym, getting in a good workout before he heads to the ice.

This early in the season, the mood in the team is light. Everyone's excited about the potential ahead. Leon skirts in and out of conversations, not wanting to get drawn too deep into anything. The only person who seems to notice anything amiss is Nuge, who pulls him aside after practice.

"All good?" he asks.

"Yeah, just a bit tired. Didn't sleep well," Leon says.

Nuge stares at him with his all-seeing eyes. He's probably the most perceptive guy on the team, quiet, always watching and listening without drawing too much attention to himself.

"You want to come over later? Bre wants to get the BBQ out one last time before we retire it for the year."

"I can't," Leon says, scrambling for an excuse he can give. "I've got someone coming over later. Broken faucet in my guest bathroom."

Great. Really believable stuff.

Nuge squints at him as if he's trying to figure out if Leon is bullshitting him or, more likely, has already figured out Leon is bullshitting him and debating the merits of calling him out.

Leon fidgets on his blades, rocking from side to side. He's aware he's being short with Nuge, that his continued silence will only make him look more suspicious, but this is all he's got today. After a few seconds of consideration, Nuge nods at him and lets Leon go without further fuss.

After practice, Leon talks to the media, giving them nothing by dry sound bite after dry soud bite. It's too early in the season for them to have anything to pick on, so they mostly ask him about the upcoming game, what he's looking forward to.

"Seattle's a good team. It will be a tough game, but we're ready for them." Leon says.

He wonders how many times in his life he's given the same answer, word by word. At least a few dozen, he reckons, but the journalists nod along like he's said something meaningful. Easy crowd. Let's see what they say after the game.

A lot of the guys head out after practice, but Leon decides to stick around. He could go home to have lunch and his nap there, but they have some decent couches in the common rooms, and Leon is not in the mood to deal with his bad life decisions at home.

The game is at least a good distraction. Leon gets to yell in people's faces, does a body-check, gets two minutes in the penalty box, and then comes out and does it again. He's always liked the physicality of hockey, how you can let your body move without needing to think about it, and that game is no exception.

All in all, it's not until midnight that he makes it back home, tired but at least not too frustrated after a solid win at home.

He's greeted in his mudroom by Connor, who opens the front door for him the minute Leon's got his boots off.

"Hey," Leon says. Connor looks nearly identical to what he'd looked like earlier, but there's something frantic to his movements now. He wrings his hands together as he looks at Leon.

"Hi. I wasn't sure what time you would come in," he says.

"Oh, sorry about that," Leon says. He didn't think to give Connor a heads-up about his schedule. Doesn't even know how to do so. Does Connor need a phone? "I can get you a phone."

Connor shakes his head. "I have your number. I can call you, but you didn't—" he stops himself, leaving Leon in silence.

"Didn't what?"

"Didn't say if it was okay for me to call you at your job?"

"Of course it is. Or you could just text. If you can text," he adds. He has no idea how Connor's able to do calls or texts without a phone. Same way he has access to GPS, he supposes. He's not human after all.

"I can text," Connor says.

Leon would expect him to relax a little now that they got that out of the way, but Connor still looks nervous as he follows Leon inside and into the kitchen. It's a bit like having a nervous dog following him around, if he happened to own a dog with big green eyes and the build of a hockey player.

"I was going to cook your favourite meal for you, but then I realised you didn't have any ingredients, and you already have a bunch of meals in your fridge. So then I was going to heat one of those up, but I didn't want it to be cold by the time you returned, which means in the end I didn't do anything. I'm sorry."

"Hey, hey, it's okay. Seriously. It's all good. I already ate at the rink," Leon says. He doesn't add that he usually heats a second meal at home, feeling bad that he stressed out Connor. He grabs a Gatorade from his fridge and sits down at his kitchen island, Connor sitting at his side.

"Oh, okay. That's good."

Connor nods at him. Leon nods back.

"Yeah. I'm actually pretty tired, so I might just go to bed. Is there anything else you need? Like, do you need… pyjamas? A change of clothes? I know you don't sweat, but—"

"I do sweat."

Leon blinks at him. "You do?"

"Yes, I—" Connor stops, looks off to the side before looking back. "Did you not read the full brochure when you bought me?"

Now Leon is the one who struggles to meet his eyes. "Not really. It was sort of an impulse decision."

"Oh. Okay."

"Sorry," Leon says as he rubs the back of his neck. God, how many more times can he make an ass of himself?

"Well, I do sweat. Most humanoid robots don't, but my model is supposed to be realistic for your enjoyment, so I am able to replicate most human behaviour. I can sweat, spit, come and piss. I don't, huh," Connor pauses, looks away for a second before looking. "Excrete. Most people find it off-putting. Plus, it makes things easier between us if I don't, so they make it so my waste comes out as a liquid. It's not like I have anything solid to get out anyway."

It's a lot of information to take in at once, but the thing Leon fixates on is Connor himself, the way he pauses as he speaks, how his eyes dart from side to side as if he's struggling to look at Leon. The way he looks embarrassed, and Leon believes it to be true, the emotion as genuine on him as it would be on a real human being.

"Lots of fluids, got it. Does that mean you have to drink water?"

"A little. Just enough to keep myself lubricated."

Okay, well, isn't that an interesting sentence? Leon's mind starts to wander to all the different ways Connor means that, what lubrication could mean to a robot that does not need to follow human behaviour, then decides to chop the thought before it wanders. He has enough on his plate as it is right now.

"I can give you some of my clothes for now. We can go shopping later if you'd like."

Connor starts nodding before Leon has even finished speaking. "Your clothes are fine. I'm not picky."

"Good, good. Okay," Leon says. He's glad they got all this out of the way. He can sort some clothes for Connor, maybe set up a credit card for him to get some stuff for his room.

He's thinking about what else Connor might need when Connor scootches his chair closer, their knees bumping into one another.

"So do you want…" Connor leaves the sentence unfinished. He licks his lips, a motion Leon is drawn to in the otherwise stillness of the room.

"What?" Leon asks.

Connor puts a hand on Leon's thigh. "Me."

"Oh. Huh. I just." Leon puts his hand on top of Connor's but makes no move to pull it in further. "I don't know. Do you want to?"

The question makes Connor lean back in his chair, his hand falling back to his side. "Do I want to?" Connor asks.

"Yeah. Do you want to have sex with me?" Leon asks. He feels awkward in a way he hasn't since he was a teenager, even though Connor is not doing anything outrageous. Leon has been propositioned plenty of times before. Between the hockey and his looks, he's never struggled at drawing people in.

He's not sure why the fact that it's a robot doing it is throwing him off his game, but the whole situation feels off.

Connor takes a few seconds to puzzle the question, his head turning slightly to the side as he eyes Leon. "I want to make you happy. That's my prime directive."

"Your prime directive?" Leon asks. Connor looks at him like he's stupid, which, yeah, fair enough.

"Yes. My reason for being. The literal reason why I was created. I, Connor, was made to make you, Leon, happy. I have limitations, rules, things I can't do, like…" Connor looks frustrated, like he's having to drag the explanation out of a closed recess of his mind.

"Like what?"

"Like murder. Causing harm to another human being. The laws of robotics, I can't go against any of those, but this," he puts his hand on Leon's thigh again. "This, I can do."

It's not the most appetising of offers, it must be said. Murder, sex with Leon. Potato, potahto.

"Well, that's not what I want," Leon says, feeling spiteful. He crosses his arms over his chest and looks away from Connor.

"It's not?" Connor asks.

"No, I don't want to have sex with someone—or something, or whatever—who doesn't want to have sex with me. That's not what I'm into."

"But I do want to have sex with you!" Connor argues.

"No, you're just saying that because it's what you've been programmed to say. You just compared sex with me to murder."

"I— that's not— I did not say that!"

Leon sighs, drained more by this conversation than from a chat with the media after a bad loss. They're going around in circles. He wonders if it's a language or a human-robot barrier, if there's anything he can say that will make sense to Connor. Regardless, he feels inept, unable to make his point, unsure whether he even has a point to make or if he's a bit too dejected to deal with this whole thing.

"Listen, I'm tired. I'm gonna go to bed now. I'll give you some pyjamas, and then you can go to your room and get some rest. We can talk more in the morning, yeah?"

Connor doesn't look particularly pleased by this idea, but he doesn't protest it either, so Leon gets up and heads out. He leaves a pair of pyjamas by Connor's door, then goes through his own nighttime routine. He takes a couple of melatonin pills before crawling into bed, but ruins their effect by getting on his phone and scrolling through social media for a couple of hours.

Now that's more in tune with the world of humanoid robots, he's surprised by the amount of content he sees with them. It seems more people own bots than he previously thought, though most are not nearly as advanced as Connor. It seems most bots are either smart enough to hold a conversation, but clunky and slow, with extremely limited movement, or they're agile but with little sentience.

He does see a couple of videos with bots similar to Connor, who look and talk like humans. The content is mostly what you'd expect. People flirting with bots, touching them, and making them pose for the camera. Leon feels gross even looking at them. There's something about the lack of agency that is a real turn-off for him.

It makes him wonder why he even got Connor in the first place, if he can't view him as anything but unwilling property.

Turning off his phone and trying to get some sleep doesn't help him. He's pretty sure he spends the whole night awake, but he must get some rest because he opens his eyes at one point to the sound of his alarm clock, 8 am sharp on the dot.

He goes downstairs to find Connor once more sitting on the couch, staring at the stairs as he waits for him. He's wearing the same clothes from yesterday and has a placid look on his face, as if he's been on standby mode the whole time he waited for Leon to wake up.

"Good morning," Leon says, having to clear his throat a couple of times to clear his voice.

Connor stands up to greet him. "Good morning. Did you sleep well?"

"Yeah, great. You?" he asks, before remembering that Connor does not, in fact, sleep. "Charging went okay?" he amends.

"It was adequate," Connor replies. "Do you want breakfast? Should I get that done for you?"

Leon shakes his head. "I can sort myself out," he says, heading to the kitchen. He hears Connor pad in behind him, light on his feet.

Leon is surprised to find the space a lot cleaner than he remembers leaving it. He has a housekeeper, but they only come on Friday mornings, which means Connor must have done it.

"You don't have to clean up after me," Leon says.

"Why not?"

"It's just not your job," Leon huffs.

From across the room, Connor crosses his arms and stares at him. "Okay, so you don't want to have sex with him, you don't want me to cook, and you don't want me to clean. Is there anything you want from me?"

"Look, I don't know, okay?" Leon puts his hands down on the counter, hanging his head. "Maybe this whole thing was a mistake. Maybe it would be best if I sent you back."

"Sent me back?" Connor asks.

"There's a return window, isn't there? I'm pretty sure it said so on the website," Leon says. It's been a while since he looked at it, and he'd hardly done more than skim it at the time, but he's quite sure he read something in those lines.

HAPPY RESULTS GUARANTEED, YOUR MONEY RETURNED OTHERWISE! WE TAKE CREDIT CARD!

Connor looks a little lost for words. Leon has to give it to the engineers who built him. It looks exceedingly convincing. His hands are clenched into fists, and he shakes his head a few times as if to clear his thoughts.

"But then they'll turn me off. They'll wipe my hard drive," he says.

Leon has no clue what any of that means. "Like, they'll give you a new job?"

Connor actually rolls his eyes at him, which is rather rude. For a robot that's been built to make Leon happy, he sure argues a lot.

"No, they're not going to give me a new job, because I'm not some generic model they can just resell. I was made for you. That means I'm no good for anyone else. Not my thoughts, not my body. They'll wipe me clean, break me into parts and then re-use them for something else."

"Oh," Leon says.

"Yeah," Connor agrees. He runs a hand through his hair and looks at Leon again with his big green eyes. It could be a trick of the light, but Leon could swear he sees a wet reflection there.

The realism is just—it's crazy. If he didn't know any better, he would say Connor looks genuinely upset, but that's insane. Connor is a robot. He shouldn't have any hard feelings about what Leon does or does not do. He's only acting, replicating human emotions. It's very convincing, though.

"Listen, we don't have to make any rash decisions," Leon finds himself saying. He doesn't know where he's going with this, what he's doing. The only thing he knows is that he doesn't want to be responsible for his robot's untimely demise. Leon has enough bad karma on his shoulders as it is. "You can stay."

His words are like a switch. Connor's big green eyes are clear of tears, and he flashes Leon a gigantic smile. "You sure? Because that would be great. Really great," he says, taking a step towards Leon. "I promise, I'll make it worth your money."

"It's not about the money," Leon sighs, hiding his face behind his hands. God, what's he gotten himself into? "I've got lots of money. Having lots of money is what got me into this mess in the first place."

Connor doesn't say anything in response, but after a few seconds of silence, Leon feels a gentle grip pull his hands away from his face. "Well, I promise to make it worth it to you anyway."

"You don't have to. I'm serious."

"But I want to," Connor says, stepping even closer. He leans in until he can rest his head on Leon's neck, choreographing his movements slowly as if he's testing the waters, giving Leon time to react. "I told you, I want this."

Leon doesn't want them to have the same argument again, but the words slip out before he can stop them. "You've just been programmed to say that."

Connor makes a noise of displeasure, but doesn't step away. He puts his hands on Leon's hips, pulling them close until they're flush with one another. Leon is shocked by how warm Connor is, his whole body radiating heat. He feels a kiss on his neck, featherlight.

"Haven't we all been programmed, in a way? Your programming is your genetics. They've determined your looks, your core traits, and your temperament. My programming is in lines of code, but why should that mean less?"

Leon's pretty sure it's not that simple, but he doesn't know enough about human genetics and robot engineering to win the argument. Connor is also fairly distracting. His hands sneak underneath Leon's t-shirt as he sucks a kiss onto Leon's neck, biting him in the spot where Leon is most sensitive.

"You need to tell me if I do something you don't like, okay? You can't just roll with it because I want it," Leon says, aware he's not even making sense. Connor was built for him. There's probably nothing Leon can ask for that he wouldn't happily give. Still, he needs to say the words, if not for Connor then for himself.

"Sure," Connor agrees, as if the request means nothing to him. He lifts his head and smiles at Leon.

Leon's not sure why he's surprised to find kissing Connor is just as good as kissing anyone else, maybe even nicer. Connor's lips are soft, his mouth insanely warm. He kisses with the confidence of someone who knows what they're doing, pulling Leon's bottom lip between his teeth to get him to open up.

His big hands paw at Leon's chest, pushing his shirt up and out of the way as his nails scratch skin. Leon finds himself reciprocating on automatic, grabbing the hem of Connor's shirt and pulling until Connor takes it off. Connor's not heavily muscled, but he's fit, his body trim with almost no body fat.

Leon watches as Connor gets down on his knees and mouths Leon's dick through the boxers. Even through the fabric, Connor's mouth is so warm and wet, it makes Leon shudder. Connor looks up at him, making eye contact the entire time he pulls down Leon's boxers. He only closes his eyes when he starts to blow Leon properly, as if he's enjoying himself so much he's lost in his pleasure.

The sight alone is enough to drive Leon a little crazy, but it's still nothing compared to the sensation of Connor's mouth on him, his throat opening so easily, perfectly made to take Leon's cock. He's good at this, of course he is, able to take all of Leon in his mouth and using his hands to roll over his balls, applying exactly the right amount of pressure.

Leon holds on to Connor's hair and comes so quickly that he would be embarrassed if it weren't so good.

It takes him a few seconds to catch his breath, and when he does, Connor is back on his feet, one of his hands shoved down his own trousers. Leon grabs his wrist without thinking and Connor instantly stills.

"Do you not want me to?" he asks.

Leon's reaching for him before he's even finished speaking. "I do, I do." He bumps one of Connor's hands out of the way so he can do it instead, enjoying how it makes Connor's eyelids flutter. His cheeks are this ridiculous shade of red that Leon is, for some reason, weirdly attracted to.

Connor presses his face into Leon's neck, panting as he gets closer, the noises so sweet they make Leon start to harden again in his jeans. Connor wraps his arms around Leon's shoulders, pulling them even closer as he comes.

"Thank you," Connor says after.

Leon shakes his head, but doesn't correct Connor. He doesn't want any more arguments on what Connor should and shouldn't do.

"No worries," he says, feeling like an idiot.

Leon takes a step back and looks down at his hand. Connor's come looks pretty normal. Curiosity gets the best of him, and Leon licks across his palm. He's surprised to find it tastes mostly watery with a hint of lemon.

"I can customise the taste if you don't like it," Connor says, which is only marginally less weird than saying 'thank you' after coming.

"It's not bad," Leon tells him. It's actually kind of nice. Fresh, even. He's about to ask Connor if he wants to take a shower when Connor makes a high-pitched sound and freezes. "Hey, Connor. You alright?"

Connor does not reply. His eyelids flutter, this time not from enjoyment but in a freaky, mechanical way, blinking non-stop. Leon reaches for Connor's hand and finds it so stiff it's immovable.

What is he supposed to do? Leon reaches for his phone at the same time as he remembers Connor's instruction manual. He bolts out of the kitchen, but when he gets me to the living room, he can't find the booklet anywhere. Neither can he find the container Connor came in, which means Connor must have moved it at some point when he cleaned up. He's just about to run to Connor's room when a voice calls behind them.

"Leon."

Leon spins on his feet, nearly tripping. "You're back."

"I am sorry I made you worry. A new update came in while we were… together. It caused a minor malfunction, but I've been able to fix it."

"Oh, good. So you're okay?"

"I'm functioning adequately, yes," Connor says. He smiles at Leon, who cringes in response.

It was easy to pretend when he was wrapped up in the moment that Connor was a real person, someone warm and sweet and good. Seeing him like this, Leon is reminded of his own foolishness. Connor's not real. None of this is real. It's just lines of code, written to make him happy, the proof that enough money can buy you anything if you're desperate enough.

"Good," Leon replies, his breath coming in a little short. He feels a tingling in his hands, a tightness across his chest. "I'm gonna go take a shower now."

"Do you want me to—"

"No," Leon barks. "You've done enough."

He turns around and flees for the stairs, taking them two at a time.

Leon's done a lot of stupid shit in his life, but this? This has got to be the stupidest, and there's nothing he can do to fix it. He can't go back on his word and throw Connor to the garbage, and he can't pretend he's real either.

Why couldn't he be normal and blow all his money in a strip club like the other guys?

Leon hops in the shower with a groan, still mad at himself. He turns the water up to a boil. It takes a minute, but he finds himself unwinding as the water beats his sore muscles.

His only mercy is that at least nobody knows about Connor. He'll have to figure out a plan when his family come to visit, but they're not planning anything for at least another year or so.

The thought makes him relax further. He's almost back to his regular self when he hears his doorbell ring, followed by the sound of his front door opening.

"Hello," he hears Connor say before Leon has the chance to yell at him to stop.

Leon grabs his towel and jumps out of the shower. Hopefully it's a delivery driver or something and not—

"Hello, is Leon home?"

After nearly ten years sharing a locker room, the voice is unmistakable. Nevertheless, Leon still holds on to the blind hope that he's wrong, and it's some stranger standing on his front steps and not one of his teammates.

He's really not sure why he's trying to lie to himself though, because when he reaches his front hall in nothing but a towel, water dripping down his hair, he of course finds none other than Ryan Nugent-Hopkins taking off his coat and shoes.

Both he and Connor turn to Leon at the same time. Connor with a smile, Nuge with a look of confusion.

"Hey man. And who's this?" he asks, giving Connor a friendly grin.

"No one," Leon says, at the exact same time as Connor replies.

"I'm Leon's."

Leon watches as both of Nuge's eyebrows climb up his forehead.

God. Fucking. Dammit.
